mnemonics: P(X,Y) = P(X AND Y): Remember that a joint probability distribution is simply the probability that two events, X AND Y, happen together.

Marginalize by Summing Out: To find the probability of just one variable (e.g., P(X)), sum (or 'marginalize') over all possible values of the other variable (Y), like adding up a row or column in a table.

Conditional is Joint over Marginal: The formula for conditional probability P(X|Y) = P(X,Y)/P(Y) can be remembered as 'The probability of X given Y is the probability of both (Joint) divided by the probability of the given condition (Marginal Y).'
keyPoints: The joint PMF, P(X=x, Y=y), gives the probability that random variable X takes the specific value x and random variable Y takes the specific value y simultaneously.
Marginal probability distributions (P(X) or P(Y)) are found by summing the joint distribution over all values of the other random variable.
Two discrete random variables are independent if and only if their joint PMF factors into the product of their marginal PMFs for all x and y: P(X=x, Y=y) = P(X=x)P(Y=y).
commonMistakes: Confusing joint probability P(X,Y) with conditional probability P(X|Y). Remember, joint is 'AND' (both happen), while conditional is 'GIVEN' (one happens assuming the other is true).
Incorrectly assuming independence. Just because two variables are uncorrelated does not mean they are independent. Always check if P(X,Y) = P(X)P(Y) holds for every single combination of x and y.
Forgetting to ensure the joint PMF sums to 1 over all possible pairs (x, y). This is a fundamental property of any probability distribution.
